title: Buttermilk scones
subtitle: Tall, flaky scones with a light, tender crumb
excerpt: Classic British scones made with buttermilk for a tender crumb and a slight tang. The key is cold butter worked in quickly and minimal mixing: overworked scone dough bakes flat and tough.
sourceNotes: Scones appear in British baking literature from the eighteenth century, with early forms closer to a griddle cake. The risen, oven-baked form using baking powder became standard in the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives a scone recipe consistent with the modern form. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), documents regional variations across Scotland, Wales, and northern England; the buttermilk version is particularly associated with Ireland and Scotland.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled scones. Defrost and warm in a low oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day they are baked. The texture is noticeably better fresh.

content.text: Two rules for tall scones: cold butter and gentle handling. Cold butter, worked in quickly by rubbing between the fingers, leaves small lumps of fat in the dough. In the hot oven those lumps steam and push the layers apart. Overworking the dough melts the butter into the flour, the layers disappear, and the scones bake flat. Mix only until the dough just comes together; a slightly shaggy, uneven dough bakes better than a smooth one.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. Stir in the sugar. Add the cold butter and rub in with fingertips until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs with a few pea-sized lumps remaining. Make a well in the centre, pour in the buttermilk, and stir with a fork until the dough just comes together. It should be soft and a little sticky; do not over-mix.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat out gently to about 3 cm thick. Do not use a rolling pin; pressing with the palm of your hand preserves the layers better. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Re-pat the trimmings once and cut again. Place on the prepared tray, touching each other very slightly for a soft side. Brush the tops with beaten egg. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes, until risen and deep golden on top. Cool on a rack for 5 minutes before splitting.
